title: RegExp Basics 🛡️
description: An introduction to Regular Expressions for bulletproof text matching.

slides.title: The Advanced Search 🕵️‍♂️
slides.content:
# The Limits of .includes()

We know how to search a string for a literal word like `text.includes("Apple")`.

But what if we are building a user signup page, and we need to check if they typed a valid email address? We can't use `.includes("email")`.

We need a way to search for **Patterns**. We need to check if the text contains "some text, an @ symbol, some text, a dot, and a domain".

This is done using **Regular Expressions (RegExp)**.

slides.title: What is RegExp? 👽
slides.content:
## The Alien Language

Regular Expressions are essentially a tiny, hyper-dense mini-language built right into JavaScript specifically for identifying text patterns.

It looks terrifying (like `/[A-Z0-9]/ig`), but it is incredibly powerful.

Instead of searching for a literal word, RegExp searches for *ideas*: "Are there numbers?", "Are there symbols?", "Does it end in 'ly'?"

slides.title: Writing RegExp ✍️
slides.content:
## Forward Slashes

Instead of using quotation marks `" "` to build a string, Regular Expressions are built using two forward slashes `/ /`.

Then, we use the `.test()` method on the RegExp object itself to test our string!

```javascript
// This RegExp checks if ANY number 0-9 exists in the text
let numberPattern = /[0-9]/;

console.log( numberPattern.test("Hello") ); // False
console.log( numberPattern.test("Hello 10") ); // True
```

slides.title: Try It Out 💻
slides.content: See how we can use RegExp to force the user to include at least one Number in their password!

quizData.question: What symbols wrap around a Regular Expression literal in JavaScript?
quizData.explanation: If it is wrapped in quotes, it is a normal string. If it is wrapped in forward slashes / /, JavaScript knows it is dealing with advanced RegExp.

slides.title: Chapter Summary! 🎉
slides.content: ## You Finished Chapter 8! You're a text manipulation wizard! You've learned: * Strings act like Arrays under the hood. * How to find text using `.indexOf()` and `.includes()` * How to chop up and extract text using `.slice()` and `.split()` * How to gracefully inject data using ES6 Template Literals \` \` * How to pattern match using RegExp `/ /` Up Next: Chapter 9! We finally escape the console and learn how to manipulate the actual Browser window and HTML DOM! 🌐
